audioscript.text: Section 1, you'll hear a young student asking the social organizer of his school for information about organized trips.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You'll see that there is an example which has been done for you.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: On this occasion only, the conversation relating to this will be played first.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I understand that the school organizes... umm, trips to different...
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, we run 5 every month.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: 3 during weekends and two Wednesday afternoon trips.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: There are five trips every month, so 5 has been written in the notes.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now we shall begin.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You should answer the questions as you listen, because you will not hear the recording a second time.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Good morning.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I understand that the school organizes... umm, trips to different...
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, we run 5 every month.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: 3 during weekends and 2 Wednesday afternoon trips.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: What sort of places?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, obviously it varies, but always places of historical interest.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And also which offer a variety of shopping, because our students always ask about that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And then we go for ones where we know there are guided tours, because this gives a good focus for the visit.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Um. Do you travel far?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, we're lucky here obviously, because we're able to say that all our visits are less than 3 hours drive.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: How much do they cost?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh, again it varies.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Between 5 and 15 pounds a head, depending on the distance.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh huh. Oh, and we do offer to arrange special trips, if you know there are more than 12 people.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, right, I'll keep that in mind.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And what are the times normally?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We try to keep it pretty fixed, so that that students get to know the pattern.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We leave at 8:30 am and return at 6:00 pm.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: We figure it's best to keep the day fairly short.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And how do we reserve a place?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You sign your name on the notice board.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Do you know where it is?
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh huh. I saw it this morning.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And we do ask that you sign up 3 days in advance, so we know we've got enough people interested to run it, and we can cancel if necessary with full refund of course.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's fine, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 5 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And what visits are planned for this term?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Right, well, I'm afraid the schedule hasn't been printed out yet, but we have confirmed the dates and planned the optional extra visits.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Which you can also book in advance if you want to.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, that's all right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: If you can just give some idea of the weekend ones, so I can, you know, work out when to see friends, et cetera.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Oh sure.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Well, uh, the first one is Saint Ives.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's on the 13th of February, and we'll have only 16 places available, uh, cause we're going by minibus.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And that's a day in town with the optional extra of visiting the Hepworth museum.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: All right, yeah, that sounds good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. Then there's a London trip on the 16th of February.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And we'll be taking a medium sized coach, so there'll be 45 places on that.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And let's see the optional extra is the tower of London.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, I've already been there.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Uh. After that, there's Bristol on the 3rd of March.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Where?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Bristol, BRISTOL.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's in a different minibus with 18 places available.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, and the optional extra is a visit to the SS Great Britain.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We're going to Salisbury on the 18th of March.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And that's always a popular one, because the optional extra is Stonehenge.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: So we're taking the large coach with 50 seats.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And then the last one is to Bath on the 23rd of March.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh yes, is Bath the Roman city?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that's right.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And that's in the 16 seater minibus.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: And where's the optional visit?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's to the American Museum, well worth a visit.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK, well that's great.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Um. Thanks for all that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: My pleasure.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Oh, by the way, if you want more information about any of the trips, have a look in the student newspaper.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Or have a word with my assistant.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: Her name is Jane Yentob.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That's YENTOB.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: All right, I've got that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Thank you very much for all your help.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You're very welcome.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: I hope you enjoy the trips.
audioscript.speaker: WOMAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

test_text.title: Riverside Industrial Village: History and Site Overview

audioscript.text: Section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You'll hear a tour guide giving a talk about a museum center called Riverside Industrial Village.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 13.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 13.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good afternoon, everybody, and welcome to Riverside Industrial Village.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To start your visit, I'm just going to give you a brief account of the history of the museum.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before letting you roam about on your own, I won't keep you long, OK?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, from where we're standing, you've got a good view of the river over there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it was because of this fast flowing water that this site was a natural place for manufacturing works.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The water and the availability of raw materials in the area like minerals and iron ore.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And also the abundance of local fuels like coal and firewood, all made this site suitable for industry from a very early time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Water was the main source of power for the early industries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And some of the water wheels were first established in the 12th century, would you believe?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At that time, local craftsmen first built an iron forge just behind the village here on the bend in the river.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By the 17th and 18th centuries, the region's rivers supported more than 160 water mills, and many of these continued to operate well into the 19th century.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But then the steam engine was invented, and then the railways came, and the centers of industry were able to move away from the rivers and the countryside and into the towns.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So industrial villages like this one became very rare.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, that's the history for you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you'd like any more information, you can ask me some questions, or you can read further in our excellent guidebook.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 14 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 14 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, I'm going to give you a plan of the site, and I'd just like to point out where everything is, and then you can take a look at everything for yourself.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I've already pointed out the river.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which is on the left, and of course, running along the bottom is Woodside Road.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Got it?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, now we're standing at the entrance, see it at the bottom, and immediately to our right is the ticket office.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You won't need that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because you've got your group booking, but just past it are the toilets, always good to know where they are.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In front of us is the car park, as you can see.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And to the left, by the entry gate is the gift shop.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's where you can get copies of the guide like this one here.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, beyond the car park, all the buildings are arranged in a half circle with a yard in the middle.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The big stone building at the top is the main workshop.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's where the furnace is, and where all the metal was smelted, and the tools were cast, as you'll be able to see.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now in the top right-hand corner, that building with bigger windows is the showroom.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Where samples of all the tools that were made through the ages are on display.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the top left corner is the grinding shop, where the tools were sharpened and finished, and on one side of that you can see the engine room, and on the other is the cafe, which isn't an antique.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You'll be pleased to know, though they do serve very nice old-fashioned teas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The row of buildings you can see on the left are the cottages.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These were built for the workers towards the end of the 18th century, and they're still furnished from that period, so you can get a good idea of ordinary people's living conditions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Across the yard from them, you can see the stables, where the horses were kept for transporting the products, and the separate building in front of them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Is the works office, and that still has some of the old accounts on display.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Right, if anyone wants a guided tour, then I'm starting at the engine room.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you'd like to come along this way, please, ladies and gentlemen.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of section 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: I've already pointed out the river.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Which is on the left, and of course, running along the bottom is Woodside Road.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Got it?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, now we're standing at the entrance, see it at the bottom, and immediately to our right is the ticket office.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You won't need that.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because you've got your group booking, but just past it are the toilets, always good to know where they are.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In front of us is the car park, as you can see.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And to the left, by the entry gate is the gift shop.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's where you can get copies of the guide like this one here.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, beyond the car park, all the buildings are arranged in a half circle with a yard in the middle.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The big stone building at the top is the main workshop.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That's where the furnace is, and where all the metal was smelted, and the tools were cast, as you'll be able to see.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now in the top right-hand corner, that building with bigger windows is the showroom.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Where samples of all the tools that were made through the ages are on display.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In the top left corner is the grinding shop, where the tools were sharpened and finished, and on one side of that you can see the engine room, and on the other is the cafe, which isn't an antique.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: You'll be pleased to know, though they do serve very nice old-fashioned teas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The row of buildings you can see on the left are the cottages.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These were built for the workers towards the end of the 18th century, and they're still furnished from that period, so you can get a good idea of ordinary people's living conditions.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Across the yard from them, you can see the stables, where the horses were kept for transporting the products, and the separate building in front of them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Is the works office, and that still has some of the old accounts on display.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Right, if anyone wants a guided tour, then I'm starting at the engine room.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you'd like to come along this way, please, ladies and gentlemen.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of section 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to section 3.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

desc.constraint: NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS AND/OR A NUMBER

items: Riverside Village was a good place to start an industry because it had water, raw materials and fuels such as <input[]> and <input[]>.
The metal industry was established at Riverside Village by <input> who lived in the area.
There were over <input> water-powered mills in the area in the eighteenth century.
body.title: Riverside Industrial Village

audioscript.text: Section 3, you'll hear Melanie, a student, talking to one of her lecturers about her studies.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 27.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 27.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Excuse me. Dr Johnson.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: May I speak to you for a minute?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Sure, come in.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: I'm Melanie Griffin.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I'm taking your course in population studies.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Right, uh well, melanie, how can I help you?
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: I'm having a bit of trouble with the second assignment.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Hmm. And it's due in 12 days.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: What sort of trouble are you having?
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Is the assignment question a problem?
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Well, that's part of the problem.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I'm also having, I've been having trouble getting hold of the books.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: I've been to the library several times, and all the books are out.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Sounds like you should have started borrowing books a bit earlier.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Well, I had a really big assignment due in for another course, and I've been spending all my time on that, and I thought.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Uh. You might get an extension of time to finish your assignment for me.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: If that's possible, but I don't know...
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Well, yes, it is possible, but extensions are normally given only for medical or compassionate reasons.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Otherwise, it's really a question of organizing your study, and we don't like giving extensions to students who simply didn't plan their work properly.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Uh. What did you get for your first assignment?
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: I got 87 percent.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Hmm, yes, you did very well, indeed.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: So obviously you can produce good work.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: I don't think I'll need too much extra time, as long as I can get hold of some of the important references.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Well, since you did so well in your first assignment.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: I'm prepared to give you an extra two weeks for this one, so that'll mean you'll need to submit it about a month from now.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Oh, thank you.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Now, what about the reading materials?
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Have you checked out the journal articles in the list?
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Um, uh. No, not yet.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: There were about 20 of them, and I wasn't sure which ones would be most useful or or important.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Well, they're all useful.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: But I don't expect anyone to read them all, because a number of them deal with the same issues.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Uh. Let me give you some suggestions.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: The article by Anderson and Hawker is really worth reading.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Right, I'll read that one.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: You should also read the article by Jackson.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Uh. But just look at the part on the research methodology, how they did it.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Jackson, got that.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: And if you have time, the one by Roberts says very relevant things, although it's not essential.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: So, OK, if it's useful, I'll try and read that one.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Now the one by Morris.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Uh. I wouldn't bother with that at this stage, if I were you.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: OK, I won't bother with Morris.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Oh, now someone told me the article by Cooper is important.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Well, yes, in a way.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: But just look at the last part where he discusses the research results.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: And lastly there's Forster.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: I can't think why I included that one.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: It's not bad, and could be of some help, but not that much.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 28 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 28 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now let's deal with the assignment question.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Uh. What's the problem there?
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: It's the graph on page 2.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: What seems to be the problem?
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: It's just the bar graph showing reasons why people change where they live.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Well, I've got a photocopy, but the reasons at the bottom are missing.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Oh, OK.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Uh. Look at the first bar on the graph.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Now that indicates the number of people who move because they want more space.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Oh, I see.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Bar 1, OK.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Now what about the next bar?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Bar 2 is to do with the people living nearby disturbing them, so they chose to move away to somewhere quieter.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Now let's look at bar No. 3.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Another reason people change their place of living is because they want to be closer to the city.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: OK, proximity to the city is an issue.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Now bar No. 4 refers to problems when the owner of the property won't help fix things that go wrong.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: In other words, the owner is not helpful.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: And so the tenants move out.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: OK, now what about bar 5?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Uh. Bar five is about those people who move because they need a bus or train to get them into the city or to go to work.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: OK, and bar six?
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Bar No. 6 is interesting.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: That reason was given quite a lot, people moving because they wanted to be in a more attractive neighborhood.
audioscript.speaker: DR JOHNSON

audioscript.text: Oh, yes.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: Thank you very much.
audioscript.speaker: MELANIE

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers. Now turn to Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

text: What recommendation does Dr Johnson make about the journal articles?

test_text.title: The Impact of Trees on the Urban Landscape

audioscript.text: Section 4, you'll hear a talk on the subject of the urban landscape.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good day, ladies and gentlemen.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I have been asked today to talk to you about the urban landscape.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are two major areas that I will focus on in my talk.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: How vegetation can have a significant effect on urban climate, and how we can better plan our cities using trees to provide a more comfortable environment for us to live in.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Trees can have a significant impact on our cities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They can make a city as a whole, a bit less windy, or a bit more windy, if that's what you want.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They can make it a bit cooler, if it's a hot summer day in an Australian city, or they can make it a bit more humid, if it's a dry inland city.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On the local scale, that is in particular areas within the city, trees can make the local area more shady, cooler, more humid, and much less windy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, trees and planting of various kinds can be used to make city streets actually less dangerous in particular areas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: How do trees do all that, you ask?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, the main difference between a tree and a building is a tree has got an internal mechanism to keep the temperature regulated.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It evaporates water through its leaves, and that means that the temperature of the leaves is never very far from our own body temperature.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The temperature of a building surface on a hot sunny day can easily be 20 degrees more than our temperature. Trees, on the other hand, remain cooler than buildings because they sweat.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This means that they can humidify the air and cool it, a property which can be exploited to improve the local climate.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Trees can also help break the force of winds.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The reason that high buildings make it windier at ground level is that as the wind goes higher and higher, it goes faster and faster.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When the wind hits the building, it has to go somewhere.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some of it goes over the top, and some goes around the sides of the building, forcing those high level winds down to ground level.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That doesn't happen when you have trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Trees filter the wind and considerably reduce it, preventing those very large strong gusts that you so often find around tall buildings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another problem in built up areas is that traffic noise is intensified by tall buildings.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By planting a belt of trees at the side of the road, you can make things a little quieter, but much of the vehicle noise still goes through the trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Trees can also help reduce the amount of noise in the surroundings, although the effect is not as large as people like to think.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Low frequency noise in particular just goes through the trees as though they aren't there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Although trees can significantly improve the local climate, they do however take up a lot of space.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are root systems to consider.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And branches blocking windows and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It may therefore be difficult to fit trees into the local landscape.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There is not a great deal you can do if you have what we call a street canyon.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A whole set of high rises enclosed in a narrow street.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Trees need water to grow, they also need some sunlight to grow, and you need room to put them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you have the chance of knocking buildings down and replacing them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then suddenly you can start looking at different ways to design the streets and to introduce...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of Section 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: That is the end of the listening test.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Section 4, you'll hear a talk on the subject of the urban landscape.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Good day, ladies and gentlemen.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: I have been asked today to talk to you about the urban landscape.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are two major areas that I will focus on in my talk.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: How vegetation can have a significant effect on urban climate, and how we can better plan our cities using trees to provide a more comfortable environment for us to live in.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Trees can have a significant impact on our cities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They can make a city as a whole, a bit less windy, or a bit more windy, if that's what you want.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They can make it a bit cooler, if it's a hot summer day in an Australian city, or they can make it a bit more humid, if it's a dry inland city.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: On the local scale, that is in particular areas within the city, trees can make the local area more shady, cooler, more humid, and much less windy.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, trees and planting of various kinds can be used to make city streets actually less dangerous in particular areas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: How do trees do all that, you ask?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, the main difference between a tree and a building is a tree has got an internal mechanism to keep the temperature regulated.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It evaporates water through its leaves, and that means that the temperature of the leaves is never very far from our own body temperature.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The temperature of a building surface on a hot sunny day can easily be 20 degrees more than our temperature. Trees, on the other hand, remain cooler than buildings because they sweat.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This means that they can humidify the air and cool it, a property which can be exploited to improve the local climate.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Trees can also help break the force of winds.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The reason that high buildings make it windier at ground level is that as the wind goes higher and higher, it goes faster and faster.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When the wind hits the building, it has to go somewhere.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Some of it goes over the top, and some goes around the sides of the building, forcing those high level winds down to ground level.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That doesn't happen when you have trees.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Trees filter the wind and considerably reduce it, preventing those very large strong gusts that you so often find around tall buildings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another problem in built up areas is that traffic noise is intensified by tall buildings.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: By planting a belt of trees at the side of the road, you can make things a little quieter, but much of the vehicle noise still goes through the trees.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Trees can also help reduce the amount of noise in the surroundings, although the effect is not as large as people like to think.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Low frequency noise in particular just goes through the trees as though they aren't there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Although trees can significantly improve the local climate, they do however take up a lot of space.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are root systems to consider.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And branches blocking windows and so on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It may therefore be difficult to fit trees into the local landscape.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There is not a great deal you can do if you have what we call a street canyon.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A whole set of high rises enclosed in a narrow street.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Trees need water to grow, they also need some sunlight to grow, and you need room to put them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If you have the chance of knocking buildings down and replacing them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then suddenly you can start looking at different ways to design the streets and to introduce...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of Section 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have half a minute to check your answers.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: That is the end of the listening test.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
